text: Welcome to this video which documents how to make the ports for microlabectomy.
This is a right upper labectomy.
The arm is at the bottom of the screen and I'm standing anteriorly.
I'm making the first port.
That's a 5mm incision.
I then just very briefly take a diathermy
Not to diathermy all the way down but I'm just feeling exactly where the intercostal spaces are.
Now this is the important part of how to get safely into the chest.
With a 5mm port I use a clear central trocar in a 5mm
port and a 5mm straight camera.
I then importantly connect the CO2 up so as the port enters the chest the CO2 is going to push the lung away.
I do this under direct vision so I'm looking at the screen and this is the view you get.
You initially see the soft tissue, you then see the intercostal spaces and then directly ahead you see the lung coming into view.
Once you've safely introduced the port you can now take the central trocar out and place the camera in for further viewing of the chest.
This is even safe in adhesions as you can see when the lung does not come away from the chest wall.
I then change the straight camera to a 30 degree camera.
I've got an interchangeable camera cover which I find very useful.
It's also very useful if one of the cameras isn't working quite so well.
So I change to a 30 degree and now I'm going to look straight down.
You can see the gentleman's nipple and I'm now going to look down to the subxiphoid region.
So I'm now going to feel where his xiphistanum is.
and I'm going to make an incision directly below the xiphisternum.
Although we'll remove the lobe from this port, I don't want it to be too large.
It has to be just big enough for me to pass a finger under the xiphisternum.
We'll increase it later.
But if you make it too large initially, then you'll get loss of CO2 and you won't get a good seal.
I've made the incision, I'm feeling the xiphy sternum and now I'm making a diathermy incision directly down the linear alba.

It's important not to go off to the sides as you'll then get bleeding from the rectus abdominis but it's very easy to feel the linear alba and you know when you've got through because you can now pass your finger
below the xiphy sternum and up behind the sternum itself.
This is the internal view and you can see you can very quickly get into the chest.

Now it's quite important to make a decent space here because sometimes the fatty tissue just above the diaphragm can get in the way of the port and sometimes I use a Roberts or Endo Roberts just to increase this, especially in higher BMI people.
So I've now got a really good track and so I'm going to insert my 12mm sub-xiphoid port and do this under direct vision.
The CO2 pushes the diaphragm down so that's what really makes it safe and stops you going sub-diaphragmatic.
It's getting a decent amount of CO2 in the chest and I use between 5 and 8mm of mercury pressure.

So I make two more 5mm ports and I try and sort of emulate slightly the standardized anterior approach so this will be my camera port and I don't tend to diather me into the chest just a 5mm incision and then push the port through the intercostal spaces and it is under vision under camera vision so I can see that I'm going safely in and I'm not causing any bleeding
So those are our ports.
We have three 5mm ports in the intercostal spaces and a 12mm sub-siphoid port.
So now we're at the end of the operation.
I'm just finishing off by placing this right upper lobe in an endobag.
I'm going to now show you how to remove a sample through the sub-siphoid incision.
Obviously it was previously just a 12mm incision.
We're going to have to increase the length of the incision.
First of all exteriorise the mouth of the bag so that there can be no spillage.
And then I'm increasing the size of the incision in the skin.
And then the important part to increase is the incision along the linea alba.

Now the real advantage of sub-siphoid is that there are no ribs above and below, so the muscles are going to separate in all directions, which actually does make it a lot easier to get the sample out.
And the only incisions you do need to make is incisions in the linear alba, and then also when you pass your finger up into the chest, make a little bit of space.
But it is really very straightforward.
So now we're pulling the sample out.
You can see the soft tissues are expanding in all directions and it comes out really quite easily.
And you can also see by this it was quite a decent size lobe.
I'm now going to have a good feel of it because the patient had colorectal mets.
And that's how you do a subsyphoid removal.
I hope this has been helpful.
I hope this gets you to have a go.
And please get in contact and tell me how you find it.
Thank you very much for watching.
